title: Climate change and central banking: macroeconomic challenges and evidence from Africa
summary: This BIS Papers No. 169 by Ehlers, Gambacorta, and Pancotto examines how climate-related shocks affect monetary policy and financial stability, with particular focus on African economies. Physical risks (weather events, infrastructure damage) and transition risks (climate policy shifts) create both supply and demand side pressures affecting output and inflation. Simulations using the BIS-Multisector model show heterogeneous macroeconomic effects within policy-relevant horizons, with economies highly exposed to energy-intensive and agricultural sectors experiencing stronger inflationary pressures and deeper output contractions.
key_points: Physical risks from climate change (more frequent severe weather events) disrupt production, damage infrastructure, and generate both supply and demand side pressures affecting output and inflation
Transition risks from climate policy, technological shifts, and market changes can tighten financial conditions and alter relative prices
African economies are particularly vulnerable because climate shocks propagate rapidly through food and energy channels due to greater sectoral exposure and limited fiscal/insurance buffers
BIS-Multisector (BIS-MS) model simulations show climate shocks generate heterogeneous macroeconomic effects within policy-relevant horizons
Economies with higher exposure to energy-intensive and agricultural sectors experience stronger inflationary pressures and larger policy rate adjustments
The authors highlight the need to strengthen data and modelling frameworks to better account for climate-related risks in monetary policy and financial stability analysis
JEL classification: E37, E52, E58, G28, Q54
why_it_matters: This research provides quantitative evidence that climate shocks are material macro catalysts that require policy responses. Central banks and policymakers need to incorporate climate risk into their analytical frameworks as the paper demonstrates these shocks drive significant inflation and output effects within policy-relevant timeframes. African economies serve as an early indicator of vulnerabilities that could manifest more broadly in emerging markets.
macro_context: Climate-related shocks function as macro catalysts that propagate through food and energy channels, generating inflationary pressures and output contractions. The paper's BIS-MS model simulations suggest these shocks can require policy rate adjustments, implying climate events can shift the monetary policy calculus. Economies highly exposed to agricultural and energy sectors—including several African countries—face amplified effects due to greater sectoral exposure and limited fiscal/insurance buffers.
market_structure_context: While the paper focuses on macroeconomic transmission channels, its findings have market structure implications: sovereign bond yields in climate-exposed economies face elevated risk premiums, commodity markets (especially agricultural and energy) exhibit heightened volatility around climate shock events, and central bank frameworks incorporating climate risk may shift expected policy reaction functions.

issues_found: Only abstract content (279 words) available in source body; full 28-page paper with detailed model outputs not accessible for verification of specific quantitative claims
market_structure_context field is empty, though the record could note that climate risk analysis affects market pricing of sovereign debt and commodity markets
